coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of Forsythia@carbon quantum dots with natural anti-wood rot fungus activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoqi
Wang, Lei
Ren, Shiming
Hu, Zheng
Wang, Yamei
coredata.dc:description: This study synthesized the Forsythia@carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in one-pot method with a natural anti-wood rot fungus activity. Chinese herbal medicine-Forsythia was used as a raw material, and the environmental-friendly fluorescent Forsythia@CQDs was prepared through microwave treatment. The antifungal test against two fungi (G. trabeum, C. versicolor) demonstrated that the Minimum Inhibition Concentration (MIC) of Forsythia@CQDs was about 1% of the MIC of Forsythia suspense extract. The weight loss rate of wood samples treated by Forsythia@CQDs was as low as 1 (±0.5)%. The antifungal property was equivalent to that of the traditional chemical preservative Ammoniacal Copper Quats (ACQ). The leachability test results of wood preservatives showed that Forsythia@CQDs had a higher fixation rate in wood than Forsythia, and even exceeded ACQ preservatives. Also, its easily-detectable fluorescence effect could track the distribution of preservatives in the wood. This research indicates that Chinese herbal medicine@CQDs-derived environmentally-protective wood preservatives can be an improvement of standard chemical preservatives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forsythia@CQDs
Chinese herbal medicine
Antifungal property
Environmentally-protective
Wood preservatives

The authors would also like to thank the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFD0600904).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The Fluorescence of Forsythia@CQDs. b) The TEM images of Forsythia@CQDs. Inset was the particles size distributions of Forsythia@CQDs. c-d) The corresponding high resolution TEM. e) The XPS spectra of Forsythia@CQDs. f-h). The high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS spectra of Forsythia@CQDs. i) The UV − Vis spectra of Forsythia. j) The UV − Vis spectra of Forsythia@CQDs. k) The Raman spectrum of Forsythia@CQDs. l) The TGA curve of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia. m) The DSC of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia. n) The FTIR of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia. o) The XRD pattern of Forsythia@CQDs modified with broad diffraction peaks observed and centered at 2θ = 27.9°. p) The price and antiseptic performance of Forsythia@CQDs compared with other wood preservatives.

a) The SEM images of Pinus sylvestris (transverse section; radial section; tangential section). b) Fluorescence pattern of wood tissue impregnated with Forsythia@CQDs under inverted fluorescence microscope. c) The CIE color coordinates of Forsythia@CQDs. d) AFM image of Forsythia@CQDs. e) The digital photos of wood impregnated with Forsythia@CQDs under UV light.

a) Diameter of inhibition zone of C. versicolor dense inoculated with Forsythia poisonous medium and Forsythia@CQDs poisonous medium. b) Diameter of inhibition zone of G. trabeum dense inoculated with Forsythia and Forsythia@CQDs poisonous medium. c) The picture showed the concentration corresponding to the same antifungal effect of Forsythia and Forsythia@CQDs. a The initial diameter of the fungal inoculum was 5 mm; Set five groups of parallel tests for each concentrations, repeat five times for each group of data, and take the average.

a-b) Absorption and retention of the preservatives after impregnation of the wood testing blocks. Black horizontal lines represent the mean ratio. a: Forsythia@CQDs; b: Forsythia. c) Weight loss rate of wood blocks of Forsythia and Forsythia@CQDs with different impregnation concentrations. d) Digital photo of wood after 12 weeks of corrosion resistance test. a Set nine groups of parallel tests for each concentration, repeat five times for each group of data, and take the average.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of Forsythia@carbon quantum dots with natural anti-wood rot fungus activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Sandy Shrubs Fibrosis and Energy Development and Utilization, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Art Design
Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Sandy Shrubs Fibrosis and Energy Development and Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Art Design, Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Sandy Shrubs Fibrosis and Energy Development and Utilization, Inner Mongolia Agricultural University, Hohhot 010000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: NO.306, Zhaowuda Road, Saihan, Hohhot, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study synthesized the Forsythia@carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in one-pot method with a natural anti-wood rot fungus activity. Chinese herbal medicine-Forsythia was used as a raw material, and the environmental-friendly fluorescent Forsythia@CQDs was prepared through microwave treatment. The antifungal test against two fungi (G. trabeum, C. versicolor) demonstrated that the Minimum Inhibition Concentration (MIC) of Forsythia@CQDs was about 1% of the MIC of Forsythia suspense extract. The weight loss rate of wood samples treated by Forsythia@CQDs was as low as 1 (±0.5)%. The antifungal property was equivalent to that of the traditional chemical preservative Ammoniacal Copper Quats (ACQ). The leachability test results of wood preservatives showed that Forsythia@CQDs had a higher fixation rate in wood than Forsythia, and even exceeded ACQ preservatives. Also, its easily-detectable fluorescence effect could track the distribution of preservatives in the wood. This research indicates that Chinese herbal medicine@CQDs-derived environmentally-protective wood preservatives can be an improvement of standard chemical preservatives.

ce:section.ce:para: Wood is a capillary material, which has the characteristics of moisture absorption and desorption [1–3]. It is also an elastic-plastic material, which can absorb energy and resist earthquake, so that the wood structure building has a better antiseismic function [4]. Due to the susceptibility to decay and biodeterioration, wood is often counteracted by preservative substances [5,6], which strive to protect the wood from insects, fungi, and mycete [7–9]. Chinese herbal medicine (Traditional Chinese medicine is mainly composed of natural botanicals [roots, stems, leaves, fruits], which have the effect of curing diseases and health care) extracts have certain antifungal effects as wood preservatives. Forsythia is a famous Chinese medicine, and its main chemical compositions include diterpenoid compounds, 6 lignans, pinoresin, pinoresin-4-O-β-glucoside, epipinoresinol, phillygenin, Forsythin, anwulignan, and triterpenoids [10,11]. Forsythia suspense has a good inhibitory effect on fungi [12,13], and a certain antiseptic effect as a wood preservative (Forsythin is the main antifungal compound in Forsythia suspense extract) [14], but most of the effective antifungal components of Chinese herbal medicines easily lost and inactivated [15,16], and its price is slightly higher than chemical agents [17]. Chinese herbal medicine extracts as wood preservatives are seldom studied and limited to the selection of the extraction process and the screening of agents.

Carbon nanomaterials have attracted a great attention due to their unique physical and chemical structures and properties. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel carbon nanomaterials [18–20] with the characteristics of strong fluorescence, wide wavelength continuous excitation, excellent electron acceptor and donor [21–23], outstanding water solubility, and low biological toxicity. The characteristics of surface atoms are the main factors that determine these overall properties. Both the shell and bulk lattice of the surface of the atoms may contribute to the macroscopic performance of CQDs. Depending on the function of CQDs, the exact properties may be mainly determined by one or two factors. For example, surface-dominant properties such as in chemical/biosensors rely on surface charge carrier processes, and in many other cases, the lattice properties of CQDs play the most important role. Among them, the functional design of CQDs includes many routes. Under certain circumstances, we can achieve enhanced performance or new functions by choosing a suitable strategy [24–26]. The microwave method can effectively improve its fluorescence performance, and CQDs with different heteroatom doping and different group modification can be obtained by changing the synthetic substrate and preparation method, so that it has special functions and properties. By preparing CQDs modified with amino or carboxyl groups on the surface with specific compounds, and then using chemical reactions to modify them, functionalized CQDs with special properties (gene delivery, photosensitivity, fluorescence imaging) and antibacterial activity can be obtained. Surface passivation and excited-state fluorescence will affect its antibacterial activity more, and positively charged fluorescent CQDs show the strongest fungicidal activity [27,28].

To date, there are many researches on the antibacterial of CQDs [29–31]. However, there are few studies on the antifungal properties of fungi. Although the anti-corrosion performance of some Chinese herbal medicines reaches the highly resistant class, the cost is too high. On the other hand, cheap Chinese herbal medicines have poor antiseptic effects. Therefore, as a wood preservative, Chinese herbal medicine has no breakthrough in ensuring low prices and good preservative effect.

In this paper, a green and environmentally friendly Forsythia suspense (a Chinese herbal medicine with a certain antifungal effect) and CQDs with a good biocompatibility and potential antibacterial properties were used as a raw material to prepare wood preservative. We succeeded to prepare Chinese herbal medicine into CQDs for the first time, which could double the antifungal properties and reduce the price of wood preservatives. Also, the leaching resistance in the treated wood could be improved, the environmental protection performance of plant-derived preservatives was retained.

Ultra-purewater (Reach chromatographic purity level; Resistance value: 15) was purchased from Beijing China Pingan Water Purification Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Carbamide (CH4N2O), Ethanolamine (C2H7NO), Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), Citric Acid (C6H8O7), Glucose (C6H12O6), Agar ((C12H18O9)n), and Ethanol (C2H5OH)) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Hohhot, Inner Mongolia, China). Forsythia (test material) was purchased from Hohhot national pharmacy (Hohhot, Inner Mongolia, China). Deionized water was used for all experiments. All reagents were analytically pure and could be used without any purification.

The samples were Pinus sylvestris and Poplar. Samples with dimensions of 20 mm × 20 mm × 10 mm (Radial × Tangential × Longitudinal), 20 mm × 20 mm × 5 mm (R × T × L) were prepared for decay tests in accordance to Chinese Standard GB/T13942.1–2009 (2009c) [32]. The trees were purchased from Inner Mongolia Wooden Building Materials Co., Ltd. (Hohhot, Inner Mongolia, China). The treated Pinus sylvestris and Poplar blocks were dried at 103 °C and 24 h in the drying oven. The experimental strain, G. trabeum (Gloeophyllum trabeum (Pers.) Murrill) and C. versicolor (Coriolus versicolor) were purchased from the institute of Forest Ecological Environment and Protection, caf. (Beijing, China), and then activated by laboratory culture.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (FEI Tecnai F 20, United States) and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) (Bruker Dimension ICON, Germany) were used to observe the size, shape, and surface roughness of the Forsythia@CQDs. For TEM imaging, the Forsythia@CQDs samples were placed dropwise onto a super-thin carbon film. AFM (Spin coating method: Deposit-Dip-Spin-coating) was equipped with Nanosensors probes. All measurements were performed in air at room temperature. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (AFS-8500, Shanghai, China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra (Bruker Dimension ICON, Germany) were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Liquid film method: drop 1–2 drops of the liquid sample between two round salt du slices to form a thin liquid film, and then put it into the light path for detection). The successful synthesis and functionalization of Forsythia@CQDs had also been confirmed by various other characterization methods. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was tested on the diffractometer covering the entire sample table (X'Pert PRO MPD, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used for regular full-spectrum narrow-spectrum testing of Forsythia@CQDs (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, United States). For liquid samples, the sample was dropped on the silicon wafer and dried layer by layer to reach the test thickness. Raman was used to analyze molecular structure (LabRam HR Evolution, France). Thermal gravimetric (TGA) was used to analyze the weight loss rate of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia with increasing temperature, and its DSC data was simultaneously given (TA, Netzsch, Germany). UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) was used to measure the absorbance of Forsythia@CQDs at different wavelengths (UV-2600/2700, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence of Pinus sylvestris plant tissues impregnated with Forsythia@CQDs was observed by a fluorescence microscope (inverted fluorescence microscope: Olympus, model: ix73, Japan). Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (JSM-7900F, Japan) was used to observe the size and shape of Forsythia.

72.0000 g powdered Forsythia (80 mesh) was dried in an oven at 80 °C and then dissolved in 100 mL ethanol with microwave extraction at 300 W power for 2 min, by repeating 3 times [33]. Subsequently, the extraction was further extracted by decompression and filtration and prepared into a concentration of 8 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 40 mg/mL, 60 mg/mL by rotary evaporation (speed: 80 r/min, temperature: 80 °C, vacuum degree: 0.04 MPa). The MIC was determined through the inhibition zone experiment.

Adding different concentrations of Forsythia suspense extractive to make poisonous culture medium before PDA medium was cooled on the ultra-clean workbench. A sterile perforator was used to cut the 5 mm diameter dense block of G. trabeum and C. versicolor into the middle part of the culture medium after the medium cooled and coagulated, and then placing it in the incubator [temperature (28 ± 2 °C), air relative humidity 80%] for 7 days. The diameters of the fungal circles of the blank control group and the toxic medium added with Forsythia suspense extract were recorded daily from day 3.

Firstly, different amines (ethanolamine (C2H7NO) and ethylenediamine (C2H8N2)) were selected, and different processing process of Forsythia suspense extractive (60 mg/mL) was added to the prepared PDA medium, respectively. Secondly, the poisoned medium was poured into a sterilized Petri dish (diameter of 9 cm). Finally, the mycelium block was inoculated into the middle of the medium and the potato medium was solidified and cultured for 7 days and recorded the diameter of the mycelium. According to the bacteriostatic circle experiment, method (e) had the most obvious effect (Fig. S1).
There are many different methods for synthesizing CQDs [34,35], and the top-down microwave synthesis method with a fast synthesis rate and short reaction time was selected in this experiment.

The Forsythia@CQDs was prepared by solvothermal carbonization of ethanolamine (C2H7NO), carbamide (CH4N2O), and Forsythia suspense at the same reaction temperature. Typically, the synthesis of water-soluble Forsythia@CQDs was performed in 10 mL conical flask using a HXG-T225 Microwave Reaction System (0–4800 W step-less adjustable, working frequency: 2450 MHz) equipped with 1000 W power. Briefly, Forsythia@CQDs was synthesized using 1.0000 g carbamide and dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure water under a conical flask, followed by the addition of 1 mL Forsythia of 60 mg/mL and 0.2000 g the respective amine (ethanolamine) to obtain a total volume of 10 mL. The synthesis was performed by a microwave reaction system at 90 °C, with a heating time of 5 min and a microwave power of 500 W. Compared with other synthesis methods, the reaction time is shortened, and the fluorescence properties can be improved subsequently. The synthesis and surface passivation of CQDs were completed by the one-step method. The solution changed from brown to dark brown solid particles, indicating the formation of carbon nanoparticles. Synthesized Forsythia@CQDs was left for 5 min to cool at room temperature. The obtained Forsythia@CQDs was centrifuged at a speed of 4000 r/s and a time of 3 min. The supernatant obtained by centrifugation was freeze-dried. Forsythia@CQDs was circular with good dispersion and particle size of about 2.6 nm.

Forsythia@CQDs in the conical flask to treatment by UV light for 40 min and filter with a sterile filter on the ultra-clean workbench before mixing Forsythia@CQDs with the medium. The method of MIC was the same as above. The concentrations of Forsythia@CQDs of 1%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8% and 10% configured poisonous medium to act on brown-rot (G. trabeum), and 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12% and 14% configured poisonous medium acts on white-rot (C. versicolor). The MIC were 10% (G. trabeum) and 14% (C. versicolor), respectively.

In the experiment, 150 pieces of Pinus sylvestris (20 mm × 20 mm × 10 mm, L × R × T), 150 pieces of Poplar test specimens (20 mm × 20 mm × 10 mm, L × R × T) and 300 pieces of feeding wood (20 mm × 20 mm × 5 mm, L × R × T) with no discoloration, decay, and obvious defects visible to the naked eye were selected. Polish with sandpaper to remove burrs and surface stains and mark the numbers with a marker. The treated wood and untreated wood were tested for decay resistance according to the Chinese Standard GB/T 13942 1-2009(2009c) [32]. The untreated and treated samples were dried at 103 °C for 12 h and the dry weight of the sample was taken before decay testing.

Three groups of controlled trials: Blank, Forsythia (0.06 g/mL, 0.08 g/mL, 0.10 g/mL, 0.12 g/mL, 0.14 g/mL), Forsythia@CQDs (five groups of Forsythia@CQDs were prepared with Forsythia at concentrations of 0.06 g/mL, 0.08 g/mL, 0.10 g/mL, 0.12 g/mL, and 0.14 g/mL as raw materials). Among them, 45 of Pinus sylvestris and 45 of Poplar specimens were processed by Forsythia@CQDs, 45 of Pinus sylvestris and 45 of Poplar specimens were processed by Forsythia, 60 of Pinus sylvestris and 60 of Poplar specimens were blank control groups, 5 of concentration gradients were selected for each group of agents, and the agents were completely infiltrated into the wood after 0.5 h vacuum pressure infusion. The surface liquid was wiped off with filter paper and the wood was weighed (accurate to 0.0001 g).

Firstly, the drug-loaded samples and the blank samples were put into an oven at a temperature of (103 ± 2 °C) to dry to a constant weight and weigh, and then eight layers of gauze were wrapped with each blank samples and made the moisture content of the blank samples reach 40–60% in a steam sterilizer for 30 min. Secondly, the cooled samples (test piece on the surface of the wood) were placed on the surface of the feeding wood when the feeding wood on the surface of the culture medium in the bottle was covered by mycelium (the river sand sawdust medium according to the Chinese Standard GB/T 13942.1-2009). Finally, the culture bottles were put at a temperature of 28 °C and a humidity of 80% in a constant temperature and humidity box, the infection was about 12 weeks, and the mycelium was scraped off at the end of the culture period and weighed. The difference between the initial weight and the final weight was used to calculate the weight loss of the wood block due to fungus exposure (Eqn 1).
(1)
ML =
( w
1
-
w 2
)
w 1
× 100 %
where ML is the mass loss of sample, W1 is the dry weight of sample before decay, W2 is the dry weight of sample after decay.
Classification of fungal decay resistance is carried out for four classes: highly resistant class (0–10%), resistant class (11–24%), slightly resistant class (25–44%), non-resistant class (˃45%).

Forsythia suspense extracts were selected 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 40 mg/mL, 60 mg/mL, and 80 mg/mL to act on Poplar samples, and 8 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 40 mg/mL, and 60 mg/mL to act on Pinus sylvestris samples. Forsythia@CQDs was selected 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12%, and 14% to act on Poplar wood samples, and 1%, 2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, and 10% to act on Pinus sylvestris wood samples. 6 samples were processed for each concentration, and the samples were numbered and weighed before processing. The samples were immersed under vacuum conditions (−0.08–0.085 MPa), the treatment time was 1 h, and the sample absorbed the liquid medicine to be basically saturated. Take out the samples and wiped the surface with filter paper until there was no liquid. Then the samples were weighed to calculate the drug load. The samples were dried in an oven 103 °C to dryness and weighed, and then the loss rate test was performed in accordance to Chinese Standard GB/T 29905–2013 [36]. Calculation of the total mass of preservative components to be tested in the wood sample:

The total mass (m) of the test component of the preservative in the wood sample mass was calculated ( Eqn 2).
(2)
m = c ×
m G
× 1000
where m is the total mass of the test component of the preservative in the wood sample, in milligrams (mg), c is the mass fraction of the components to be tested in the antiseptic solution (%), mG is the mass of the liquid medicine absorbed by the sample during the antiseptic treatment, in grams (g).

The drug loading (Ret) of the test component of the preservative was calculated according to formula ( Eqn 3).
(3)
Ret = m/V
where Ret is the amount of preservative to be tested, the unit was kilogram per cubic meter (kg/m3), m is the total mass of the test component of the preservative in the wood sample, in milligrams (mg), V is the volume of the sample for anti-corrosion treatment, in cubic centimeters (cm3).

Churn method: 6 samples were put with similar drug loadings as a group, put them in a 500 mL beaker, 180 mL deionized water was added, immersed the samples below the surface of the deionized water, stirred with a stir bar, and replaced them every 6 h, 24 h, 48 h deionized water once, and then replaced with deionized water every 48 h for a total of 14 days. The filtrate of each replacement was collected and combined for content analysis.

Calculation of the Loss Rate of Preservative Components to be tested: the mass (mA) of the test component of the preservative in the filtrate is calculated based on the volume of the filtrate and the concentration of the components in the filtrate.

The loss rate (L) of the test component of the preservative was calculated according to the formula ( Eqn 4).
(4)
L =
m A
/ m × 100 %
where L is the loss rate of test component of preservative, mA is the mass of the test component of the preservative in the filtrate, in milligrams (mg), m is the total mass of the test component of the preservative in the wood sample, in milligrams (mg).

Studies have shown that the antibacterial activity of CQDs depend on their size and surface charge, which can be changed by adjusting the functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amide, amino, epoxy, etc.) on the surface of CQDs. Therefore, the experiment first tested the antifungal properties of different CQDs against fungi by adding different surface passivation, showing effective antifungal properties related to ethanolamine.

The method of (e) was the most obvious through process screening (Fig. S1). Among them, method (g) was a water-soluble CQDs synthesized by a one-pot method using citric acid as a carbon source, urea as a surface modifier, and equipped with a multiwave 3000 microwave reaction system. The concentration was 10 times that of the Forsythia extract added in (a-f), and the (a-c) methods were the microwave pre-solution of the (e-f) configuration. The results of the inhibition zone diameter showed that CQDs and Forsythia suspense extract had similar effects. Among methods (a-f), method (a) was second only to the method (e) (Fig. S1, Table S1).

The results showed that Forsythia@CQDs was successfully synthesized from Forsythia, ethanolamine, and carbamide via a one-pot method (Figure 1 ). Compared with other bottom-up CQDs synthesis routes, our experimental design used ultrapure water as an environmentally friendly solvent, used cheap starting materials, the microwave time was relatively energy-saving with only 5 min, and generated the least waste.

The newly synthesized Forsythia@CQDs was analyzed and characterized after removing all impurities by dialysis and centrifugation. For the initial detection experiments, 2 mL Forsythia@CQDs was loaded into a cuvette and the fluorescence intensity at their optimal emission wavelength was measured via excitation with their optimal excitation wavelength to obtain satisfactory detection results. Figure 1(a) displayed the excitation − emission map of the Forsythia@CQDs aqueous solution. It exhibits an excitation-dependent emission with the excitation wavelength ranging from 300 nm to 600 nm, indicating that their fluorescence process is dominated by non-uniform emission states. Furthermore, the maximum excitation of Forsythia@CQDs aqueous solution occurred at 360 nm and the optimal emission is at 436 nm. It illustrates that the prepared Forsythia@CQDs processed excellent fluorescence characteristics with blue fluorescence.

The SEM characterization results of Forsythia were shown in Fig. S2. The results showed that Forsythia was spherical, uniformly dispersed, and its particle size was about 13.3 μm. The large particle size of Forsythia makes it difficult to enter fungal cells and cannot cause serious damage to fungal cells. The morphology of as-synthesized Forsythia@CQDs was performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It clearly showed that Forsythia@CQDs were fabricated successfully (Figure 1(b-d)). TEM images demonstrated that the prepared Forsythia@CQDs were circular with an uniform dispersion with the particle size of about 2.5 nm (Figure 1(b)). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in the insets of Figure 1(c, d) indicated that the Forsythia@CQDs had similar well-resolved lattice fringes, with a spacing distance of 0.25 nm and 0.14 nm attributed to the orientation (100; 020) lattice of graphitic carbon. The results of TEM showed that Forsythia@CQDs was successfully deposited into nano-scale CQDs which could be completely immersed in wood and even wood cell walls, which would highly inhibit the growth of fungi in wood. The chemical compositions of Forsythia@CQDs were characterized via FT-IR and XPS. The surface composition and elemental valance state of the Forsythia@CQDs were detected by XPS [37]. Figure 1(e) showed the full survey XPS spectra of the Forsythia@CQDs, and the XPS spectra of Forsythia@CQDs showed three peaks at 283 eV, 398 eV and 530 eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The results revealed that the content of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen gradually increased from the Forsythia@CQDs, and their contents were 53.62%, 25.07%, and 21.32%, respectively [38]. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of the Forsythia@CQDs in Figure 1(f) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 287.4 eV, 284.3 eV, and 283.4 eV, corresponding to CO, CC, and CC, respectively. The N1s spectra in Figure 1(g) displayed two peaks at 397.9 eV, and 398.6.3 eV, attributed to NH, N, and CN/CN, respectively. In Figure 1(h), the O1s band could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.8 eV and 529.8 eV, assigned to CO and CO, respectively [39–41].

The UV − Vis absorption spectrum of Forsythia@CQDs aqueous solution revealed two broad absorption peaks at 211 nm and 275 nm originating from the π − π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains (CC, CC) and the n − π* transition of multi-conjugate CO and CO of the Forsythia@CQDs, respectively. While the raw material Forsythia had only one peak at 308 nm, which should be credited to π − π* (CC, CC) ( Figure 1(i-j)). Raman spectrums as shown in Figure 1(k) exhibited two broad peaks at 1347 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1, which were characterized by D and G bands of the Forsythia@CQDs.

TGA was employed to investigate the thermal stability of Forsythia@CQDs under nitrogen gas atmosphere. As seen in Figure 1(l), the water content on the surface of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia was in the range of 1.5–3.5 wt%. The initial thermal decomposition temperature of Forsythia@CQDs was lower than that of Forsythia at about 4 °C. When the temperature reached about 190 °C, the weight loss was caused by the decomposition of the surface carboxy group and other organic molecules of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia. According to the thermogravimetric curve, it could be seen that the thermal decomposition curve of Forsythia is almost the same as Forsythia@CQDs at 194–344 °C. When the temperature increased to 344 °C, the main body of weight loss was the irregular fracture reaction and decomposes into small molecular hydrocarbons, alkanes, and other products, accompanied by the continuous decomposition of the pyrolysis residue of Forsythia. When the temperature increased to 400 °C, the CC bond of Forsythia@CQDs was broken. When the temperature increased to 800 °C, the carbonized residue formed due to the thermal reduction of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia. Endothermic reaction of Forsythia@CQDs occurred at 240.10300 °C, 343.60300 °C, and 456.40300 °C, and Endothermic reaction of Forsythia occurred at 380.44200 °C and 426.04200 °C (Figure 1(m)).

Figure 1(n) showed the FT-IR spectra of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia. The absorption peak at 1656 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of aromatic CO, which was consistent with the Raman result and XPS result. The absorption peak at 3476 cm−1 indicated that the intramolecular association OH. These results demonstrated that the Forsythia@CQDs was composed of aromatic skeletons. The characteristic peaks at 2911 cm−1, 1701 cm−1, 1522 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, 1362 cm−1, 1238 cm−1, and 1028 cm−1 in the Forsythia were due to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO, δ NH, δ OH, Aliphatic, CO, and COC, respectively. After solvothermal carbonization, the natural carbon skeletons structure of Forsythia was preserved. CC and CO, δ NH in Forsythia were retained during the synthesis process of Forsythia@CQDs, and new chemical bonds were synthesized, such as CC, CN, CN, indicating that the structure of the internal of Forsythia had changed in the process of synthesis.

To characterize the graphitic structure of the Forsythia@CQDs, XRD pattern was employed. As shown in Figure 1(o), two prominent peaks at 27.8° and 12.6°, which corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of the graphitic structure. The peak at 27.8° was sharp, which was the (002) diffraction peak of graphitic structure. It was found that the Forsythia@CQDs had an obvious broad diffraction peak in the range of 10–30°, which was attributed to an amorphous characteristic peak of carbon, and the corresponding crystal plane was (002) crystal plane. The lattice spacing of the material is 0.38 nm, indicating that Forsythia@CQD has a sp2 hybrid carbon core of graphitic carbon. As shown in Figure 1(p), the cost of the prepared Forsythia@CQDs was significantly lower than other Chinese herbal medicine extracts and chemical preservatives with antifungal effect, and the antifungal effect was equivalent to chemical preservatives.

Figure 2(a) was the SEM of the wood. Observe the fluorescence of Pinus sylvestris plant tissues impregnated with Forsythia@CQDs with a fluorescence microscope (observe with a 10x objective lens, excited with a violet laser). It could be observed that the Pinus sylvestris after dipped showed a good fluorescence effect and the Forsythia@CQDs was evenly distributed (Figure 2b).

The CIE color coordinates of Forsythia@CQDs were (0.16559, 0.12666), (0.15466, 0.10575), (0.16568, 0.11740), (0.16744, 0.11096), (0.17260, 0.10887), (0.18669, 0.11291), (0.16384, 0.10708), (0.19548, 0.30384), (0.17115, 0.18245), (0.17548, 0.21217), (0.17115, 0.18245), (0.16628, 0.14088), (0.18412, 0.25498), CIE values of various Forsythia@CQDs-TW were blue, which was consistent with the result of fluorescence microscope (Figure 2(c)). The roughness and surface morphology of Forsythia@CQDs was observed by AFM (Figure 2(d)). Figure 2(e) showed photos of wood impregnated with Forsythia@CQDs under daylight and UV light, and photos of the blank control under daylight and UV light.

Firstly, the in vitro antifungal test and MIC test were carried out on the medium of potato, agar and glucose. The blank control group and the poisoned medium with the MIC of Forsythia@CQDs demonstrated that the prepared Forsythia@CQDs had a distinct inhibitory effect on G. trabeum and C. versicolor (Fig. S3). Then, the MIC of Forsythia and Forsythia@CQDs were determined by setting the concentration. The MIC of Forsythia suspense inhibiting G. trabeum and C. versicolor were 60 mg/mL and 80 mg/mL, respectively, and the MIC of Forsythia@CQDs were 10% and 14%, respectively (Figure 3 , Table S2, S3).

Based on the results of in vitro experiments, different concentrations of Forsythia suspense, Forsythia@CQDs, and impregnate the wood were set by vacuum pressure for a period (Figure 4 a, b). After 12 weeks of the corrosion resistance test, the quality loss of the control sample was measured to reach more than 45 (±0.5)% (Figure 4c). It could be clearly seen that the sample with Forsythia@CQDs exhibited good corrosion resistance (Figure 4d). It could be seen from the weight loss rate of wood that Forsythia@CQDs had a good bacteriostatic effect. The weight loss rate of the sample decreased with the number of antiseptic agents inhaled increased, and the weight loss rate of the samples treated with Forsythia@CQDs were significantly lower than that after Forsythia treatment of Pinus sylvestris and Poplar wood, and some even reached “1 (±0.5)%”. The mass-loss rate of white-rot fungi (C. versicolor) reached 45 (±0.5)%, and brown-rot fungi (G. trabeum) was 52 (±0.5)% among the blank samples, indicating that the brown-rot fungi’s erosion on Pinus sylvestris was far greater than that of white-rot fungi on Poplar. The antibacterial performance of Forsythia@CQDs is compared with the traditional Chinese medicine and chemical agents. Regardless of white-rot fungi or brown-rot fungi, its antibacterial properties are comparable to ACQ, and some are even better than ACQ. Concentrations of 6%-14% have reached the strong corrosion resistance level, 14% of Forsythia@CQDs has the best antibacterial performance among the sample, with a weight loss rate as low as 1 (±0.5)%, while Forsythia with the same concentration has a weight loss rate of 2 (±0.5)%.

The results illustrate that active antifungal compounds are contained in Chinese herbal medicine-derived extracts, and that most Chinese herbal medicine might have potential as anti-fungal agents, although further research will be necessary to better understand the role of increasing concentrations on performance or some of the remarkable effect of Chinese medicine to conduct an in-depth compound test. Finally, the as-prepared CQDs are used as the modification method of traditional Chinese medicine to compensate for the defects of Chinese herbal medicine extract, and the well-designed Chinese herbal medicine extraction@CQDs not only have a lower cost than chemical preservatives, but also possess a better antibacterial effect.

The size and surface modification of traditional Chinese medicine nanoparticles are considered to be the main source of fungal nanoparticles with a smaller particle size that could enter plant cells more easily, causing serious damage to fungal cells until they lose activity [42]. On the other hand, the toxicity of nanoparticles can be directly affected by changing the large surface area and surface charge of nanoparticles. The effect of antifungal agents on microorganisms is mainly through affecting the growth of mycelium, cell permeability, mitosis, cell expansion, the disintegration of cell protoplasts, and cell wall damage. In essence, the physiological, biochemical reactions and metabolic activities relate to microbial cells have been disturbed and destroyed, which ultimately leads to the inhibition of microbial growth and reproduction, and even death. The antifungal mechanism of Forsythia@CQDs is mainly by destroying the cell structure of the fungal, causing damage to the fungal cell wall structure and leaking the contents. This is reasonable because the amino groups of the Forsythia@CQDs have positive charges and the fungus is negatively charged, leading to electrostatic interaction between the fungus and the Forsythia@CQDs. Moreover, the antifungal polypeptide changes the permeability of the fungus through the membrane integration mechanism, and the phenyl groups can even pierce into the fungus, causing the death of the fungus.

In this study, the higher nitrogen content in the Forsythia@CQDs can affect the destruction of polysaccharides and reduce the degradation of lignin. Due to the nano modification of Forsythia surface, wood treated with 1% of Forsythia@CQDs had shown effective antiseptic property to wood-rot fungi. In addition, it is well known that Forsythia extract alone does not provide sufficient antiseptic properties. In the decay test, fungal hyphae spread through the pits into the lumen and began to destroy the internal structure of the wood. However, increasing the proportion of chemical agents reduced the degradation of wood cell walls and accelerated the destruction of fungal cells. Forsythia, ethanolamine, and carbamide play an important role in the synergistic effect of improving the corrosion resistance of wood. After 1% Forsythia@CQDs was impregnated, the wood had better corrosion resistance. However, it is necessary to further study the antifungal and even insecticidal effects of Forsythia@CQDs at a higher ratio. Although the exact mechanism of various toxic complications is not yet clear, the coupling of research nanoparticles and plant-derived extracts might make nanoparticles have the required size, and are biodegradable, biocompatible, and non-toxic [20,21].

The concentration of Forsythia@CQDs and Forsythia in the leaching liquor was measured by UV–Vis after determining the leachability of wood preservatives, and it was found that Forsythia@CQDs had a higher fixation rate in wood than Forsythia, and even exceeded ACQ preservatives (Table S4). As the concentration increased, the leaching liquor was generally lower. Among them, the leaching liquor increased when the preservatives loading in Pinus sylvestris and Poplar reached a certain concentration, which might be related to the saturation of the preservative loading in the wood. The anti-loss rate of Pinus sylvestris treated wood was lower than that of Poplar treated wood. This might be due to the extracts contained in the wood of Pinus sylvestris. The extract of Pinus sylvestris wood might help to fix Forsythia and Forsythia@CQDs in the wood pores.

The antifungal activity of the extracts of Forsythia was evaluated in Petri dishes inoculated with G. trabeum and C. versicolor. Fungal inhibition results showed that both fungi had similar behavior, and G. trabeum had appeared to be more sensitive to the Forsythia extracts than C. versicolor. These qualitative results had enabled assessment of the degree of antifungal activity of the Forsythia and the Forsythia@CQDs in the specimens. For the one-pot synthesis method described in this work, these results were only illustrative. The compound might undergo structural changes such as losing some of its properties or acquiring new properties after microwave treatment. However, due to the chemical bonds on the surface of CQDs and the adhesive properties of nanomaterials, they will be the main compounds that still adhere firmly to the wood and prevent leaching. In conclusion, Forsythia@CQDs is facilely synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal reaction, and their ability to function as a bactericide in the antibacterial inactivation of G. trabeum and C. versicolor inside the wood is demonstrated. Compared with untreated wood, the quality loss rate of wood treated with Forsythia@CQDs had decreased from 52 (±0.5)% to 1 (±0.5)%. Therefore, Forsythia@CQDs of nanoscale composites has been imposed as a promising candidate for efficient and sustainability antifungal usage.

Xiaoqi Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Lei Wang: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Shiming Ren: Formal analysis, Investigation. Zheng Hu: Investigation, Validation. Yamei Wang: Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (2018MS03048), and the Science and Technology Innovation Guidance Project of Inner Mongolia. The authors would also like to thank the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFD0600904).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary information is available online free of charge. It includes a schematic of the inhibition zone of medium with preservatives added with different treatment processes and digital photos of colonies on medium with different concentrations of Forsythia@CQDs. The SEM images of Forsythia. The different treatment processes, average colony diameter of wood decay fungi grown on potato dextrose agar amended with Forsythia and Forsythia@CQDs, and leachability of wood preservatives.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2021.109800.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
